topic: A student learns their thesis advisor is leaving the university
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox with the quiet finality of a closing door. Professor Albright was leaving. My stomach immediately twisted into a tight, cold knot. I reread the subject line three times, the words blurring slightly. Every sentence of my proposal, every late night spent wrestling with the data, suddenly felt exposed under a harsh spotlight. I stared at the screen, the cursor blinking mockingly on the untouched document. *What if I’m not good enough?* The thought echoed in the silent apartment, loud and accusatory. I started pacing the small space, the worn floorboards protesting beneath my hurried steps. I picked up my pen, the plastic cool against my fingertips, and began to furiously cross out entire sections of my literature review, the red ink bleeding across the pristine white paper. It was pointless. It was all going to be pointless.

[story 2]

I found myself meticulously cleaning my desk, wiping down the surface until it gleamed, a desperate attempt to impose order on the swirling chaos inside my head. The news about Dr. Evans’ departure had hit me like a physical blow, leaving me breathless and shaky. I kept catching my reflection in the darkened monitor, and the face staring back looked pale and hollowed out. *I should have known better. I should have done more.* A wave of profound inadequacy washed over me, heavy and suffocating. I walked to the window, looking out at the indifferent campus quad. The sunlight felt sharp, illuminating every perceived flaw in my academic journey. I pulled my sweater tighter around myself, a futile gesture against the chill that seemed to emanate from deep within. I decided, suddenly and sharply, that I would just pack up my notes and walk away from this entire endeavor.

[story 3]

The administrative announcement felt less like news and more like a verdict. I sat at the library table, the fluorescent lights buzzing overhead, and watched the other students move with an easy, confident purpose. My hands were trembling slightly as I gripped my lukewarm coffee mug. Every time I glanced at the empty chair where my advisor usually sat, a sharp pang of failure pierced my chest. *They’re all so capable. Why am I the exception?* I picked up my laptop, the screen reflecting back a strained expression. I began retyping my introduction, deleting sentences until the prose was sterile, devoid of any spark. My breathing became shallow, quick catches of air. I felt a burning pressure behind my eyes, a desperate need to erase the work, to undo the effort before anyone else could see the shaky foundation underneath.
